claims.claim: This paper evaluates a serving-boundary context compression strategy in which a compact summary of prior context replaces the original prompt prefix at the boundary between consecutive inference requests, retaining only the summary plus a recent token tail in the KV cache.

evidence_refs.quote: # Serving-Boundary Context Compression: KV-Cache and Prefill Cost Reduction via Prompt Summarization at the Serving Boundary

claims.notes: Historical backfill linked the claim to already published artifacts; this is not independent source revalidation.

claims.claim: We report analytical KV-cache projections across four compression scenarios (8k→1.5k, 16k→1.5k, 32k→3k, 64k→5k retained tokens) under an 8B-parameter grouped-query attention (GQA) geometry, finding KV-cache reductions of 81.25%–92.19% and prefill-work proxy reductions of 96.48%–99.39%.

evidence_refs.quote: # Serving-Boundary Context Compression: KV-Cache and Prefill Cost Reduction via Prompt Summarization at the Serving Boundary

claims.notes: Historical backfill linked the claim to already published artifacts; this is not independent source revalidation.

claims.claim: We supplement these projections with single-thread NumPy synthetic attention microbenchmarks on an aarch64 host, which confirm that decode-side attention cost scales with retained context length as expected (68× latency increase from 512 to 16,384 tokens).

evidence_refs.quote: # Serving-Boundary Context Compression: KV-Cache and Prefill Cost Reduction via Prompt Summarization at the Serving Boundary

claims.notes: Historical backfill linked the claim to already published artifacts; this is not independent source revalidation.

claims.claim: The central open question—whether compressed summaries preserve task accuracy—remains unresolved.

evidence_refs.quote: # Serving-Boundary Context Compression: KV-Cache and Prefill Cost Reduction via Prompt Summarization at the Serving Boundary

claims.notes: Historical backfill linked the claim to already published artifacts; this is not independent source revalidation.

claims.claim: First, KV-cache memory scales linearly with the number of retained prompt tokens; for an 8B-class GQA model, a single 64k-token context occupies approximately 8 GiB of KV-cache memory.

evidence_refs.quote: # Serving-Boundary Context Compression: KV-Cache and Prefill Cost Reduction via Prompt Summarization at the Serving Boundary

claims.notes: Historical backfill linked the claim to already published artifacts; this is not independent source revalidation.

claims.claim: Second, prefill attention computation exhibits quadratic dependence on prompt length, making time-to-first-token (TTFT) a growing concern for long-context workloads.

evidence_refs.quote: # Serving-Boundary Context Compression: KV-Cache and Prefill Cost Reduction via Prompt Summarization at the Serving Boundary

claims.notes: Historical backfill linked the claim to already published artifacts; this is not independent source revalidation.

claims.claim: A natural strategy is to compress the prompt at the *serving boundary*—the point between consecutive inference requests—by replacing the full prior context with a compact summary and retaining only this summary plus a recent token tail.

evidence_refs.quote: # Serving-Boundary Context Compression: KV-Cache and Prefill Cost Reduction via Prompt Summarization at the Serving Boundary

claims.notes: Historical backfill linked the claim to already published artifacts; this is not independent source revalidation.

claims.claim: KV-cache memory per request is modeled as: $$M_{\text{KV}} = n_{\text{tokens}} \times n_{\text{layers}} \times n_{\text{KV heads}} \times d_{\text{head}} \times 2 \times b$$ where the factor of 2 accounts for K and V projections and $b$ is the bytes per element (2 for fp16).

evidence_refs.quote: # Serving-Boundary Context Compression: KV-Cache and Prefill Cost Reduction via Prompt Summarization at the Serving Boundary

claims.notes: Historical backfill linked the claim to already published artifacts; this is not independent source revalidation.

claims.claim: For the reference 8B-like GQA geometry used throughout (32 layers, 8 KV heads, 128 head dimension, fp16), this yields 131,072 bytes per token.

evidence_refs.quote: # Serving-Boundary Context Compression: KV-Cache and Prefill Cost Reduction via Prompt Summarization at the Serving Boundary

claims.notes: Historical backfill linked the claim to already published artifacts; this is not independent source revalidation.

claims.claim: Prefill work is proxied as proportional to $n_{\text{tokens}}^2$ for full self-attention over the prompt, reflecting the quadratic cost of the attention computation.

evidence_refs.quote: # Serving-Boundary Context Compression: KV-Cache and Prefill Cost Reduction via Prompt Summarization at the Serving Boundary

claims.notes: Historical backfill linked the claim to already published artifacts; this is not independent source revalidation.

claims.claim: The retained token counts (1,536–5,120) represent plausible summary-plus-tail lengths; they are not derived from any particular summarization model.

claims.claim: To confirm that retained-context length is a measurable cost driver on the decode path, we implemented a single-token synthetic attention benchmark using NumPy on CPU.
